title: Percentile Distribution (P25/P50/P75/P90/P95/P99)
when_to_use: user wants quartile, decile, or percentile distribution of a metric (e.g., revenue, order_value)
user’s question includes '25th percentile', 'P95 threshold', 'top 10% customers'
comparing individuals to a distribution (e.g., 'is this order in the top quartile?')
building benchmark reports (e.g., 'customers in P90+ spend 2× more')
when_not_to_use: user wants cumulative or rolling percentiles — use window functions with CUME_DIST instead
user needs exact quantiles for small datasets (<100 rows) — exact COUNT(DISTINCT) + ranking may be clearer
distribution is highly skewed with many outliers — consider IQR-based robust methods instead

concept_explanation.what_it_does: PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) returns the exact 50th percentile (median), interpolating between rows if needed. PERCENTILE_DISC returns the actual row value without interpolation. Both give distribution anchors for classification.
concept_explanation.mental_model: Imagine a crowd lined up by height. P50 is the height where half the crowd is shorter and half taller — if two people are exactly in the middle, P50 interpolates their heights. P95 means 95% of people are shorter, 5% taller.
concept_explanation.key_behavior: PERCENTILE_CONT requires WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY ...) clause. The ORDER BY must be deterministic (no ties or use tiebreaker). Interpolation makes P50 more accurate than P50 from PERCENTILE_DISC for even-sized datasets.

template.minimal:
-- Revenue distribution: P25, P50, P75, P90, P95, P99
SELECT
  PERCENTILE_CONT(0.25) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) AS p25_revenue,
  PERCENTILE_CONT(0.50) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) AS p50_revenue,
  PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) AS p75_revenue,
  PERCENTILE_CONT(0.90) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) AS p90_revenue,
  PERCENTILE_CONT(0.95) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) AS p95_revenue,
  PERCENTILE_CONT(0.99) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) AS p99_revenue
FROM order_items
template.realistic:
-- Revenue percentiles by region, plus classify each order
-- Grain: one row per order with percentile bucket
WITH percentiles AS (
  SELECT
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.25) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) FILTER (WHERE c.region = 'North') AS p25_north,
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.50) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) FILTER (WHERE c.region = 'North') AS p50_north,
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) FILTER (WHERE c.region = 'North') AS p75_north,
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.25) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) FILTER (WHERE c.region = 'South') AS p25_south,
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.50) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) FILTER (WHERE c.region = 'South') AS p50_south,
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) FILTER (WHERE c.region = 'South') AS p75_south
  FROM orders o
  JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
  JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
  WHERE o.order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days'
),
order_metrics AS (
  SELECT
    c.region,
    o.order_id,
    oi.quantity * oi.unit_price AS revenue
  FROM orders o
  JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
  JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
  WHERE o.order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days'
),
classified AS (
  SELECT
    om.region,
    om.order_id,
    om.revenue,
    CASE
      WHEN om.region = 'North' THEN
        CASE
          WHEN om.revenue <= p.p25_north THEN 'P0-P25'
          WHEN om.revenue <= p.p50_north THEN 'P25-P50'
          WHEN om.revenue <= p.p75_north THEN 'P50-P75'
          ELSE 'P75-P100'
        END
      WHEN om.region = 'South' THEN
        CASE
          WHEN om.revenue <= p.p25_south THEN 'P0-P25'
          WHEN om.revenue <= p.p50_south THEN 'P25-P50'
          WHEN om.revenue <= p.p75_south THEN 'P50-P75'
          ELSE 'P75-P100'
        END
      ELSE 'Unknown'
    END AS percentile_bucket
  FROM order_metrics om
  CROSS JOIN percentiles p
)
SELECT
  region,
  percentile_bucket,
  COUNT(*) AS order_count,
  AVG(revenue) AS avg_revenue
FROM classified
GROUP BY region, percentile_bucket
ORDER BY region, percentile_bucket
template.composable:
-- Percentile thresholds per region, then flag orders above P90
-- Composes: stat_percentile_distribution + stat_zscore_outlier
WITH region_percentiles AS (
  SELECT
    c.region,
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.90) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS p90_revenue,
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.95) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS p95_revenue,
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.99) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS p99_revenue
  FROM orders o
  JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
  JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
  WHERE o.order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '60 days'
  GROUP BY c.region
),
order_metrics AS (
  SELECT
    c.region,
    o.order_id,
    oi.quantity * oi.unit_price AS revenue
  FROM orders o
  JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
  JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
  WHERE o.order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '60 days'
)
SELECT
  om.region,
  om.order_id,
  om.revenue,
  rp.p90_revenue,
  rp.p95_revenue,
  rp.p99_revenue,
  CASE
    WHEN om.revenue > rp.p99_revenue THEN 'P99+'
    WHEN om.revenue > rp.p95_revenue THEN 'P95-P99'
    WHEN om.revenue > rp.p90_revenue THEN 'P90-P95'
    ELSE 'Below P90'
  END AS revenue_tier
FROM order_metrics om
JOIN region_percentiles rp ON om.region = rp.region

mistake_patterns.mistake: Using PERCENTILE_CONT without WITHIN GROUP
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: PERCENTILE_CONT requires WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY ...). Without it, DuckDB throws a syntax error; Postgres returns 'syntax error at or near WITHIN'.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Query fails with 'syntax error' on PERCENTILE_CONT.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) FROM orders
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY order_total) FROM orders

mistake_patterns.mistake: Assuming PERCENTILE_CONT = PERCENTILE_DISC
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: PERCENTILE_CONT interpolates between rows; PERCENTILE_DISC returns an actual row value. For even datasets, P50 may differ by one row’s value.
mistake_patterns.symptom: P50 = 50.0 (CONT) vs 45 (DISC) — user expects exact row value but gets interpolated.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY order_total) AS median FROM orders
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT PERCENTILE_DISC(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY order_total) AS median FROM orders
mistake_patterns.detection_sql:
SELECT PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY order_total) AS cont,
       PERCENTILE_DISC(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY order_total) AS disc
FROM orders

mistake_patterns.mistake: Using percentiles on small datasets (<100 rows)
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: With <100 rows, percentile positions may be unstable. A 1-row change can shift percentiles significantly — P50 could jump by 20%.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Percentile thresholds change wildly between days — not reliable for benchmarking.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT PERCENTILE_CONT(0.90) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY order_total) FROM orders
mistake_patterns.good_sql:
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders
-- Only use percentiles if count > 100
mistake_patterns.detection_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders WHERE order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days'

mistake_patterns.mistake: Missing GROUP BY when computing region-level percentiles
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: PERCENTILE_CONT is an aggregate function — without GROUP BY, it returns a single row for the entire dataset, masking region differences.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Only one row returned — user expects multiple rows (one per region).
mistake_patterns.bad_sql:
SELECT c.region, PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY oi.quantity * oi.unit_price)
FROM orders o
JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
GROUP BY c.region
mistake_patterns.good_sql:
SELECT c.region, PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) FILTER (WHERE c.region = c.region)
FROM ... GROUP BY c.region
mistake_patterns.detection_sql:
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM (
  SELECT c.region, PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY oi.quantity * oi.unit_price)
  FROM orders o
  JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
  JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
  GROUP BY c.region
) t

dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: DuckDB supports PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY ...). It interpolates by default.
dialect_traps.postgres_behavior: Postgres supports the same syntax. It also supports quantile() for array-based estimation.
dialect_traps.bigquery_behavior: BigQuery supports PERCENTILE_CONT() OVER (ORDER BY ...) as a window function, but not PERCENTILE_CONT WITHIN GROUP as an aggregate.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: For BigQuery, use PERCENTILE_CONT() OVER (ORDER BY ...) and filter for the percentile position. For DuckDB/Postgres, WITHIN GROUP is preferred.

dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: DuckDB supports FILTER (WHERE ...) with PERCENTILE_CONT for conditional percentile computation.
dialect_traps.postgres_behavior: Postgres supports FILTER with PERCENTILE_CONT from 9.4 onward.
dialect_traps.bigquery_behavior: BigQuery does not support FILTER with aggregate functions — use CASE WHEN instead.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: For portability, use CASE WHEN: PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY CASE WHEN region = 'North' THEN revenue END).

choose_this_when: user wants *exact* distribution thresholds (P25, P50, P75, etc.)
user’s question includes 'percentile', 'P95', 'quartile', 'P99'
data has sufficient size (>100 rows)
classification or benchmarking is needed (e.g., 'top 10% customers')

choose_alternative_when.condition: user wants rolling or time-based percentiles
choose_alternative_when.why: Rolling percentiles use window functions over time

choose_alternative_when.condition: distribution is highly skewed and outliers dominate
choose_alternative_when.why: IQR is robust to outliers; percentiles can be stretched by extreme values

edge_cases.what_happens: P50 returns that row’s value; P25/P75 may return NULL or same value — undefined behavior.
edge_cases.handling: Add row count check: WHERE COUNT(*) > 1 before computing percentiles.

edge_cases.what_happens: P25/P50/P75 all return the same value — no spread in distribution.
edge_cases.handling: No issue — percentiles are equal. Use STDDEV to detect zero variance.

edge_cases.what_happens: PERCENTILE_CONT ignores NULLs — same as other aggregates.
edge_cases.handling: No change needed. But if NULLs represent missing data, consider imputation or filtering.

performance_notes.complexity: O(n log n) for sorting, O(1) for percentile lookup. Dominated by sort cost.
performance_notes.when_slow: Large datasets (>10M rows) or many percentile thresholds (e.g., P0.1 to P99.9).
performance_notes.optimization_hint: Pre-filter to relevant rows before computing percentiles. For repeated use, materialize percentiles in a CTE. Use APPROX_PERCENTILE_CONT for approximate, faster computation.

title: Histogram Bucketing with WIDTH_BUCKET
when_to_use: user wants equal-width bins for histogram (e.g., $0-$20, $20-$40, $40-$60)
user’s question includes 'bucket by $20 increments', 'equal-width bins'
building dashboards with frequency distributions (e.g., 'how many orders between $50-$100')
preparing data for plotting histograms in visualization tools
when_not_to_use: user wants equal-count bins (e.g., 100 buckets with same *count*, not same *range*) — use NTILE instead
distribution is highly skewed — consider log transformation or IQR-based bucketing
user wants dynamic bins (e.g., adaptive to data distribution) — use percentile-based thresholds

concept_explanation.what_it_does: WIDTH_BUCKET(value, min, max, num_buckets) maps value into one of num_buckets equal-width intervals between min and max. Values outside [min, max] are assigned to bucket 0 or num_buckets + 1. Perfect for equal-range histograms.
concept_explanation.mental_model: Imagine dividing a ruler into equal segments — each segment covers the same dollar range (e.g., $0-$10), regardless of how many observations fall in each segment.
concept_explanation.key_behavior: WIDTH_BUCKET uses [min, max) for buckets, so max value maps to overflow bucket (num_buckets+1). Use min - ε and max + ε or handle overflow explicitly.

template.minimal:
-- Revenue bucketing with $20 bins from $0 to $200
SELECT
  revenue,
  WIDTH_BUCKET(revenue, 0, 200, 10) AS bucket,
  (WIDTH_BUCKET(revenue, 0, 200, 10) - 1) * 20 AS bucket_start,
  WIDTH_BUCKET(revenue, 0, 200, 10) * 20 AS bucket_end
FROM orders
template.realistic:
-- Equal-width histogram of order revenue, with frequency and percentage
-- Grain: one row per bucket
WITH order_revenue AS (
  SELECT quantity * unit_price AS revenue FROM order_items
  WHERE order_id IN (SELECT order_id FROM orders WHERE order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days')
),
min_max AS (
  SELECT MIN(revenue) AS min_rev, MAX(revenue) AS max_rev FROM order_revenue
),
bucketed AS (
  SELECT
    revenue,
    WIDTH_BUCKET(revenue, COALESCE(min_rev, 0), COALESCE(max_rev, 100), 10) AS bucket
  FROM order_revenue, min_max
),
final AS (
  SELECT
    b.bucket,
    (b.bucket - 1) * ((max_rev - min_rev) / 10) AS bucket_start,
    b.bucket * ((max_rev - min_rev) / 10) AS bucket_end,
    COUNT(*) AS frequency,
    ROUND(COUNT(*) * 100.0 / SUM(COUNT(*)) OVER (), 2) AS pct_of_total
  FROM bucketed b, min_max
  GROUP BY b.bucket, min_rev, max_rev
)
SELECT * FROM final ORDER BY bucket
template.composable:
-- Equal-width buckets per region, then compare to global distribution
-- Composes: stat_histogram_bucketing + stat_percentile_distribution
WITH order_revenue AS (
  SELECT
    c.region,
    oi.quantity * oi.unit_price AS revenue
  FROM order_items oi
  JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id
  JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
  WHERE o.order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days'
),
region_min_max AS (
  SELECT region, MIN(revenue) AS min_rev, MAX(revenue) AS max_rev
  FROM order_revenue
  GROUP BY region
),
bucketed AS (
  SELECT
    r.region,
    r.revenue,
    WIDTH_BUCKET(r.revenue, rr.min_rev, rr.max_rev, 5) AS bucket
  FROM order_revenue r
  JOIN region_min_max rr ON r.region = rr.region
)
SELECT
  region,
  bucket,
  COUNT(*) AS frequency
FROM bucketed
GROUP BY region, bucket
ORDER BY region, bucket

mistake_patterns.mistake: Ignoring overflow/underflow buckets (0 and num_buckets+1)
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: WIDTH_BUCKET assigns bucket 0 to values < min, and num_buckets+1 to values > max. Users often filter these out, losing edge cases or miscounting data coverage.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Histogram misses 5% of data in the tails — bucket 0 and 6 appear but are excluded.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql:
SELECT bucket, COUNT(*) FROM (
  SELECT WIDTH_BUCKET(revenue, 0, 100, 5) AS bucket FROM orders
) WHERE bucket BETWEEN 1 AND 5
GROUP BY bucket
mistake_patterns.good_sql:
SELECT
  CASE
    WHEN bucket = 0 THEN 'underflow'
    WHEN bucket = 6 THEN 'overflow'
    ELSE CAST((bucket - 1) * 20 AS TEXT) || '-' || CAST(bucket * 20 AS TEXT)
  END AS bucket_label,
  COUNT(*)
FROM (
  SELECT WIDTH_BUCKET(revenue, 0, 100, 5) AS bucket FROM orders
) t
GROUP BY bucket
ORDER BY bucket
mistake_patterns.detection_sql: SELECT MIN(revenue) AS min_rev, MAX(revenue) AS max_rev FROM orders

mistake_patterns.mistake: Using WIDTH_BUCKET with zero range (min = max)
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: If min = max, WIDTH_BUCKET throws 'division by zero' error because bucket width = (max - min) / num_buckets = 0.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Query fails with 'division by zero' or 'invalid bucket count'.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT WIDTH_BUCKET(revenue, 50, 50, 5) FROM orders
mistake_patterns.detection_sql: SELECT MIN(revenue) AS min_rev, MAX(revenue) AS max_rev FROM orders WHERE ...
mistake_patterns.handling_sql: CASE WHEN min_rev = max_rev THEN 1 ELSE WIDTH_BUCKET(...) END

mistake_patterns.mistake: Confusing WIDTH_BUCKET with NTILE (equal range vs equal count)
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: WIDTH_BUCKET creates equal-width *ranges* — counts may vary wildly. NTILE creates equal-count *buckets* — ranges may vary. Users expect counts to match but get wide variance.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Histogram shows uneven bars (WIDTH_BUCKET) vs uniform bars (NTILE) — user expected uniform.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT WIDTH_BUCKET(revenue, 0, 100, 10) FROM orders
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT NTILE(10) OVER (ORDER BY revenue) FROM orders

mistake_patterns.mistake: Using WIDTH_BUCKET without explicit min/max bounds
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: Default min/max (e.g., from MIN(column)) may change daily, causing buckets to shift — histogram bars move, making trends hard to interpret.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Histogram looks different each day — not comparable across time.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT WIDTH_BUCKET(revenue, (SELECT MIN(revenue) FROM orders), (SELECT MAX(revenue) FROM orders), 10) FROM orders
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT WIDTH_BUCKET(revenue, 0, 1000, 10) FROM orders
mistake_patterns.detection_sql:
SELECT MIN(revenue), MAX(revenue) FROM orders WHERE order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '7 days'
-- Compare to previous week
SELECT MIN(revenue), MAX(revenue) FROM orders WHERE order_date BETWEEN CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '14 days' AND CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '7 days'

dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: DuckDB supports WIDTH_BUCKET(value, min, max, num_buckets) as a function.
dialect_traps.postgres_behavior: Postgres does not have WIDTH_BUCKET — it requires manual calculation: FLOOR((value - min) / (max - min + epsilon) * num_buckets) + 1.
dialect_traps.bigquery_behavior: BigQuery does not have WIDTH_BUCKET — same manual calculation needed.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: For Postgres/BigQuery, use: FLOOR((value - min_val) / NULLIF(max_val - min_val, 0) * num_buckets) + 1 AS bucket. Add MIN/MAX in a CTE first.

dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: WIDTH_BUCKET ignores NULL values — they return NULL, not a bucket.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: COALESCE(value, min_val - 1) or filter NULLs first to avoid losing rows.

choose_this_when: user wants *equal-width* bins (e.g., $0-$10, $10-$20)
user’s question includes 'bucket by $X', 'equal range', 'fixed-width'
data range is stable and bounded (min/max known or reasonable)
visualization needs consistent binning (e.g., dashboard histograms)

choose_alternative_when.condition: user wants *equal-count* buckets (e.g., 10 buckets with same number of rows)
choose_alternative_when.why: NTILE creates equal-count buckets; WIDTH_BUCKET creates equal-width buckets

choose_alternative_when.condition: data is highly skewed with extreme outliers
choose_alternative_when.why: IQR-based bucketing is robust to outliers

edge_cases.what_happens: WIDTH_BUCKET returns same bucket for all rows (typically bucket 1), but min = max may cause division issues if not handled.
edge_cases.handling: Add min/max check: CASE WHEN min = max THEN 1 ELSE WIDTH_BUCKET(...) END.

edge_cases.what_happens: WIDTH_BUCKET assigns bucket 0 (underflow) or num_buckets+1 (overflow). These must be handled explicitly.
edge_cases.handling: Add CASE WHEN to label underflow/overflow buckets or filter them out if desired.

edge_cases.what_happens: All values go to bucket 1 — no distribution. This is valid but rarely useful.
edge_cases.handling: Add row count or variance check before bucketing.

performance_notes.complexity: O(n) — WIDTH_BUCKET is a simple arithmetic operation. Fast even on large tables.
performance_notes.when_slow: None — WIDTH_BUCKET is very fast. Bottlenecks are usually in pre-aggregation or large intermediate tables.
performance_notes.optimization_hint: Compute min/max once in a CTE, then reuse. Avoid recalculating per row. Filter NULLs and extreme outliers before bucketing.

title: Coefficient of Variation for Metric Stability
when_to_use: user wants to compare *variability* across metrics with *different scales* (e.g., revenue vs units sold)
user’s question includes 'which region has most stable revenue?', 'compare volatility'
filtering unstable metrics (e.g., 'exclude regions with CV > 50%')
identifying seasonal or outlier-affected data
when_not_to_use: user wants *absolute* variability (e.g., std dev in dollars) — use STDDEV directly
mean is near zero — CV becomes infinite or undefined
data is heavily skewed or has outliers — IQR-based measures may be better

concept_explanation.what_it_does: CV = STDDEV(x) / AVG(x) × 100%. It measures *relative* variability — how much data spreads around the mean, regardless of scale. A CV of 50% means values typically deviate by half the mean.
concept_explanation.mental_model: Imagine two cities: City A has average rent $1,000 with STDDEV $200 (CV=20%). City B has average rent $2,000 with STDDEV $300 (CV=15%). City B has higher *absolute* variability ($300 vs $200) but lower *relative* variability.
concept_explanation.key_behavior: CV assumes mean > 0 and data is ratio-scale. Negative means invert interpretation — use ABS(mean) or absolute CV if direction doesn’t matter.

template.minimal:
-- CV per region
SELECT
  region,
  STDDEV(revenue) AS std_revenue,
  AVG(revenue) AS avg_revenue,
  ROUND(STDDEV(revenue) / NULLIF(AVG(revenue), 0) * 100, 2) AS cv_pct
FROM orders
GROUP BY region
template.realistic:
-- Stability comparison: revenue CV vs order count CV per region
-- Grain: one row per region
WITH daily_metrics AS (
  SELECT
    c.region,
    DATE_TRUNC('day', o.order_date) AS order_date,
    SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS revenue,
    COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) AS order_count
  FROM orders o
  JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
  JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
  WHERE o.order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days'
  GROUP BY c.region, order_date
),
region_stats AS (
  SELECT
    region,
    STDDEV(revenue) AS std_revenue,
    AVG(revenue) AS avg_revenue,
    STDDEV(order_count) AS std_orders,
    AVG(order_count) AS avg_orders
  FROM daily_metrics
  GROUP BY region
)
SELECT
  region,
  ROUND(std_revenue, 2) AS std_revenue,
  ROUND(avg_revenue, 2) AS avg_revenue,
  ROUND(std_revenue / NULLIF(avg_revenue, 0) * 100, 2) AS cv_revenue_pct,
  ROUND(std_orders, 2) AS std_orders,
  ROUND(avg_orders, 2) AS avg_orders,
  ROUND(std_orders / NULLIF(avg_orders, 0) * 100, 2) AS cv_orders_pct
FROM region_stats
ORDER BY cv_revenue_pct ASC
template.composable:
-- CV comparison: revenue CV per product, filter to stable products (CV < 30%)
-- Composes: stat_cv_stability + agg_having_vs_where
WITH daily_revenue AS (
  SELECT
    p.product_id,
    p.product_name,
    DATE_TRUNC('day', o.order_date) AS order_date,
    SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS daily_revenue
  FROM orders o
  JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
  JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id
  WHERE o.order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '60 days'
  GROUP BY p.product_id, p.product_name, order_date
),
product_stats AS (
  SELECT
    product_id,
    product_name,
    STDDEV(daily_revenue) AS std_rev,
    AVG(daily_revenue) AS avg_rev
  FROM daily_revenue
  GROUP BY product_id, product_name
)
SELECT
  product_id,
  product_name,
  ROUND(std_rev, 2) AS std_rev,
  ROUND(avg_rev, 2) AS avg_rev,
  ROUND(std_rev / NULLIF(avg_rev, 0) * 100, 2) AS cv_pct
FROM product_stats
HAVING ROUND(std_rev / NULLIF(avg_rev, 0) * 100, 2) < 30
ORDER BY avg_rev DESC

mistake_patterns.mistake: Dividing by zero (AVG = 0 or near-zero)
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: CV = STDDEV / AVG. If AVG is 0, division by zero throws an error. Near-zero AVG (e.g., $0.01) makes CV huge and meaningless.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Query fails with 'division by zero' or returns CV > 10,000% for zero-mean products.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT STDDEV(revenue) / AVG(revenue) * 100 FROM orders
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT STDDEV(revenue) / NULLIF(AVG(revenue), 0) * 100 FROM orders
mistake_patterns.detection_sql: SELECT AVG(revenue) FROM orders HAVING AVG(revenue) = 0 OR AVG(revenue) < 0.01

mistake_patterns.mistake: Using CV for non-negative metrics without ensuring positivity
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: CV assumes mean > 0. If mean is negative (e.g., profit in loss periods), CV flips sign and misleads — high CV in negative range looks like high stability but isn’t.
mistake_patterns.symptom: CV for 'profit' is 200% during losses — user expects low variability.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT STDDEV(profit) / AVG(profit) * 100 FROM orders
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT STDDEV(ABS(profit)) / NULLIF(AVG(ABS(profit)), 0) * 100 FROM orders
mistake_patterns.detection_sql: SELECT MIN(profit), MAX(profit) FROM orders

mistake_patterns.mistake: Comparing CV across datasets with different granularities
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: CV on daily data differs from CV on monthly data — daily fluctuations look more variable. Comparing daily CV to monthly CV misleads.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Region A has CV=50% daily, Region B has CV=20% monthly — user thinks B is more stable, but daily B is noisier.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql:
SELECT region, STDDEV(revenue) / AVG(revenue) * 100
FROM orders
GROUP BY region
-- vs
SELECT region, STDDEV(monthly_revenue) / AVG(monthly_revenue) * 100
FROM monthly_metrics
GROUP BY region
mistake_patterns.good_sql:
SELECT region, STDDEV(rev) / NULLIF(AVG(rev), 0) * 100
FROM (
  SELECT region, DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) AS month, SUM(revenue) AS rev
  FROM orders
  GROUP BY region, month
) t
GROUP BY region
mistake_patterns.detection_sql:
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders
WHERE order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days'
-- vs
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM monthly_metrics
WHERE month >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days'

mistake_patterns.mistake: Using CV for non-metric data (e.g., counts, categories)
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: CV is for *continuous* metrics. For counts, CV is interpretable but may be less useful than variance. For categories, CV is undefined.
mistake_patterns.symptom: CV computed for 'order_status' (e.g., 'shipped', 'returned') — nonsense value.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT STDDEV(status) / AVG(status) * 100 FROM orders
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT status, COUNT(*) FROM orders GROUP BY status
mistake_patterns.detection_sql: SELECT DISTINCT status FROM orders

dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: DuckDB supports STDDEV(x) and VAR(x) — both work for CV.
dialect_traps.postgres_behavior: Postgres supports STDDEV_POP, STDDEV_SAMP, VAR_POP, VAR_SAMP. Use STDDEV_POP for population CV.
dialect_traps.bigquery_behavior: BigQuery supports STDDEV and VARIANCE with pop/samp variants.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: Use STDDEV for CV. For population standard deviation, use STDDEV_POP if available. Most engines default to sample std dev — consistent across tools.

dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: DuckDB’s STDDEV ignores NULLs — same as AVG.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: No change needed — STDDEV and AVG handle NULLs consistently. But verify with COUNT(*) vs COUNT(column) to detect NULLs.

choose_this_when: user wants to compare *relative* variability across different scales
user’s question includes 'most stable', 'compare volatility', 'CV'
mean values are positive and well-separated from zero
data is continuous (not categorical)

choose_alternative_when.condition: user wants *absolute* variability (e.g., std dev in dollars)
choose_alternative_when.why: STDDEV alone gives units (e.g., $50) — CV gives percent (e.g., 10%)

choose_alternative_when.condition: mean is near zero or data is heavily skewed
choose_alternative_when.why: IQR is robust to outliers and doesn’t require mean > 0

edge_cases.what_happens: STDDEV = 0, CV = 0% — perfect stability.
edge_cases.handling: No issue — CV=0% is correct.

edge_cases.scenario: Mean is very small but positive (e.g., $0.01)
edge_cases.what_happens: CV becomes huge (e.g., 500%) — unstable and misleading.
edge_cases.handling: Filter out rows where AVG < threshold, or use absolute CV: STDDEV / AVG(ABS(x)).

edge_cases.what_happens: Mean may be near zero, causing CV to be large or undefined.
edge_cases.handling: Use ABS(x) if direction doesn’t matter, or compute CV separately for gains and losses.

performance_notes.complexity: O(n) — STDDEV and AVG require one scan. Fast even on large tables.
performance_notes.when_slow: None — CV is a simple aggregate. Bottlenecks are usually in data preparation (joins, filters).
performance_notes.optimization_hint: Pre-filter to relevant rows before computing CV. Materialize CV per group in a CTE if reused. Use APPROX functions for very large data.

title: Correlation and Regression in SQL
when_to_use: user wants to quantify linear relationship between two metrics (e.g., advertising spend vs revenue)
user’s question includes 'correlation between X and Y', 'slope of trend line'
building regression diagnostics (e.g., 'what’s R² for advertising spend?')
preparing data for predictive modeling (e.g., feature correlation analysis)
when_not_to_use: user suspects *non-linear* relationships — correlation only captures linear trends
data has *outliers* — correlation is sensitive to extremes
user wants *causation* — correlation ≠ causation (e.g., ice cream sales and drowning deaths both rise in summer)

concept_explanation.what_it_does: CORR(x, y) measures linear correlation (-1 to 1). REGR_SLOPE(y, x) gives the slope of the best-fit line. REGR_R2 gives R² — proportion of variance explained. All require paired data and detect linear relationships only.
concept_explanation.mental_model: Imagine a scatterplot. CORR tells you how tightly points cluster around a straight line (1 = perfect line, 0 = no line). REGR_SLOPE tells you how steep that line is.
concept_explanation.key_behavior: CORR and REGR require paired non-NULL observations. Neither implies causation — confounding variables may drive both metrics.

template.minimal:
-- Correlation and regression between ad_spend and revenue
SELECT
  CORR(ad_spend, revenue) AS correlation,
  REGR_SLOPE(revenue, ad_spend) AS slope,
  REGR_R2(revenue, ad_spend) AS r_squared
FROM advertising_metrics
template.realistic:
-- Correlation and regression by quarter: ad_spend vs revenue
-- Grain: one row per quarter
WITH quarterly AS (
  SELECT
    DATE_TRUNC('quarter', date) AS quarter,
    SUM(ad_spend) AS total_spend,
    SUM(revenue) AS total_revenue
  FROM advertising_metrics
  WHERE date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '2 years'
  GROUP BY 1
),
regression AS (
  SELECT
    quarter,
    total_spend,
    total_revenue,
    CORR(total_spend, total_revenue) OVER (ORDER BY quarter) AS rolling_corr,
    REGR_SLOPE(total_revenue, total_spend) OVER (ORDER BY quarter) AS rolling_slope,
    REGR_R2(total_revenue, total_spend) OVER (ORDER BY quarter) AS rolling_r2
  FROM quarterly
)
SELECT
  quarter,
  ROUND(total_spend, 2) AS total_spend,
  ROUND(total_revenue, 2) AS total_revenue,
  ROUND(CORR(total_spend, total_revenue) OVER (), 3) AS overall_corr,
  ROUND(REGR_SLOPE(total_revenue, total_spend) OVER (), 4) AS overall_slope,
  ROUND(REGR_R2(total_revenue, total_spend) OVER (), 3) AS overall_r2
FROM quarterly
ORDER BY quarter
template.composable:
-- Correlation matrix: spend on TV, radio, online vs revenue
-- Composes: stat_correlation_regression + seg_case_when_classification
WITH daily_spend AS (
  SELECT
    date,
    tv_spend,
    radio_spend,
    online_spend,
    revenue
  FROM advertising_metrics
  WHERE date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '90 days'
),
stats AS (
  SELECT
    CORR(tv_spend, revenue) AS corr_tv,
    CORR(radio_spend, revenue) AS corr_radio,
    CORR(online_spend, revenue) AS corr_online,
    REGR_SLOPE(revenue, tv_spend) AS slope_tv,
    REGR_SLOPE(revenue, radio_spend) AS slope_radio,
    REGR_SLOPE(revenue, online_spend) AS slope_online
  FROM daily_spend
)
SELECT
  tv_spend,
  radio_spend,
  online_spend,
  revenue,
  CASE
    WHEN corr_tv > 0.8 THEN 'strong_tv'
    WHEN corr_tv > 0.5 THEN 'moderate_tv'
    ELSE 'weak_tv'
  END AS tv_strength,
  CASE
    WHEN corr_online > 0.8 THEN 'strong_online'
    WHEN corr_online > 0.5 THEN 'moderate_online'
    ELSE 'weak_online'
  END AS online_strength
FROM daily_spend
CROSS JOIN stats

mistake_patterns.mistake: Using correlation on non-linear relationships
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: Correlation captures only *linear* relationships. If the true relationship is quadratic (e.g., revenue increases then decreases with ad_spend), correlation may be near zero even though a strong pattern exists.
mistake_patterns.symptom: CORR = 0.05 for ad_spend vs revenue — user expects high correlation, but relationship is U-shaped.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT CORR(ad_spend, revenue) FROM advertising
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT ad_spend, revenue FROM advertising ORDER BY ad_spend
mistake_patterns.detection_sql: SELECT ad_spend, revenue FROM advertising ORDER BY ad_spend LIMIT 100
mistake_patterns.handling_sql: If trend is non-linear, transform variables (e.g., LOG(ad_spend)) or use non-linear models.

mistake_patterns.mistake: Ignoring outliers in correlation calculation
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: A single outlier (e.g., one day with $1M revenue) can inflate correlation from 0.2 to 0.8 — misleadingly strong.
mistake_patterns.symptom: CORR jumps from 0.2 to 0.8 after adding one row — not robust.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT CORR(ad_spend, revenue) FROM advertising
mistake_patterns.good_sql:
WITH cleaned AS (
  SELECT ad_spend, revenue
  FROM advertising
  WHERE revenue BETWEEN 100 AND 10000
)
SELECT CORR(ad_spend, revenue) FROM cleaned
mistake_patterns.detection_sql: SELECT MIN(revenue), MAX(revenue), STDDEV(revenue) FROM advertising
mistake_patterns.handling_sql: Use IQR or Z-score to filter outliers before correlation, or use robust correlation methods (e.g., Spearman).

mistake_patterns.mistake: Confusing correlation with causation
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: High correlation doesn’t imply causation. Ice cream sales and drowning deaths are correlated (both summer-driven), but one doesn’t cause the other.
mistake_patterns.symptom: User concludes 'ad_spend increases revenue' from CORR=0.8, but other factors (seasonality, competition) may drive both.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT CORR(ad_spend, revenue) FROM advertising
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT ad_spend, revenue, date FROM advertising ORDER BY date
mistake_patterns.detection_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM advertising WHERE date IS NULL
mistake_patterns.handling_sql: Add confounder variables, use time-series models, or conduct A/B tests to establish causation.

mistake_patterns.mistake: Using REGR functions on unpaired data
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: CORR and REGR require *paired* observations. If ad_spend and revenue are in separate tables, joining without a common key creates spurious correlations.
mistake_patterns.symptom: CORR = 1.0 for ad_spend vs revenue — but they’re from different tables (Cartesian product).
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT CORR(a.ad_spend, r.revenue) FROM ad_spend a, revenue r
mistake_patterns.good_sql:
SELECT CORR(a.ad_spend, r.revenue)
FROM ad_spend a
JOIN revenue r ON a.date = r.date
mistake_patterns.detection_sql:
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM ad_spend a, revenue r
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM ad_spend JOIN revenue ON ad_spend.date = revenue.date
mistake_patterns.handling_sql: Always join on a common key (e.g., date, customer_id) to ensure paired observations.

dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: DuckDB supports CORR, REGR_SLOPE, REGR_R2 as aggregate functions.
dialect_traps.postgres_behavior: Postgres supports CORR, REGR_SLOPE, REGR_R2 in PostGIS and standard SQL. Works as expected.
dialect_traps.bigquery_behavior: BigQuery supports CORR, but REGR_SLOPE/REGR_R2 require regression models (e.g., ML.REGRESS).
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: For BigQuery, use ML.REGRESS for full regression. For simple slope, compute manually: REGR_SLOPE = (COUNT * SUM(x*y) - SUM(x)*SUM(y)) / (COUNT * SUM(x^2) - SUM(x)^2).

dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: CORR ignores rows where either x or y is NULL — only paired non-NULL values are used.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: Verify NULL counts match: COUNT(*) vs COUNT(x), COUNT(y). Filter NULLs if expected (e.g., WHERE x IS NOT NULL AND y IS NOT NULL).

choose_this_when: user wants to *quantify linear relationship* between two continuous variables
user’s question includes 'correlation', 'slope', 'R-squared', 'regression'
data is paired (same row has both x and y)
exploratory analysis is needed before modeling

choose_alternative_when.condition: user wants *non-linear* relationship or robustness to outliers
choose_alternative_when.why: Spearman correlation or IQR-based methods are more robust
A/B tests or causal inference models establish causation

edge_cases.scenario: All x or all y values are identical
edge_cases.what_happens: Variance is zero — slope and correlation are undefined (division by zero).
edge_cases.handling: Filter rows where variance is non-zero, or return NULL if slope is undefined.

edge_cases.what_happens: CORR and REGR may return NULL or unstable values. R² can be 1.0 with just 2 points.
edge_cases.handling: Add minimum sample size check: HAVING COUNT(*) >= 3.

edge_cases.scenario: Outliers in both x and y
edge_cases.what_happens: Correlation may be artificially inflated — both variables move together due to shared outliers.
edge_cases.handling: Use robust methods (e.g., Spearman correlation) or Winsorize extreme values.

performance_notes.complexity: O(n) — correlation and regression require one scan for sums and cross-products.
performance_notes.when_slow: None — correlation is fast. Bottlenecks are usually in data preparation (joins, filters).
performance_notes.optimization_hint: Pre-filter to relevant rows before correlation. Materialize paired data in a CTE. For rolling correlation, use windowed CORR.

title: Moving Average with Correct Frame
when_to_use: user wants to smooth noisy data with a simple moving average (e.g., daily revenue smoothed over 7 days)
user’s question includes '7-day moving average', 'rolling average'
building trend indicators (e.g., 'price moving average' in dashboards)
detecting anomalies relative to recent history
when_not_to_use: user wants *exponential* moving average (EMA) — SMA treats all points equally, EMA weights recent points more
user needs *centered* moving average (e.g., for smoothing peaks) — this requires looking ahead and behind
user wants to avoid *warm-up period* (first N rows have incomplete averages)

concept_explanation.what_it_does: AVG(column) OVER (ORDER BY date ROWS BETWEEN N PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) computes a simple moving average (SMA) over the last N+1 rows. It’s a 'trailing' average — the current row is included in the average. The frame defines which rows contribute to each average.
concept_explanation.mental_model: Imagine dragging a window of fixed size across time — each day, the window moves one day forward, and the average is computed over the days currently inside the window.
concept_explanation.key_behavior: SMA includes current row in the window. For 'previous N days excluding today', use ROWS BETWEEN N PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING.

template.minimal:
-- 7-day moving average of revenue
SELECT
  order_date,
  revenue,
  AVG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS sma_7d
FROM daily_revenue
template.realistic:
-- Daily revenue with 7-day and 30-day moving averages, and anomaly flags
-- Grain: one row per day
WITH daily_revenue AS (
  SELECT
    DATE_TRUNC('day', o.order_date) AS order_date,
    SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS revenue
  FROM orders o
  JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
  WHERE o.order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '60 days'
  GROUP BY 1
),
with_ma AS (
  SELECT
    order_date,
    revenue,
    AVG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS sma_7d,
    AVG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 29 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS sma_30d
  FROM daily_revenue
)
SELECT
  order_date,
  revenue,
  sma_7d,
  sma_30d,
  CASE
    WHEN revenue > 2 * sma_7d THEN 'spike'
    WHEN revenue < 0.5 * sma_7d THEN 'dip'
    ELSE 'normal'
  END AS flag
FROM with_ma
template.composable:
-- 7-day moving average, then compare to 30-day moving average for trend detection
-- Composes: stat_moving_average + stat_zscore_outlier
WITH daily_revenue AS (
  SELECT
    DATE_TRUNC('day', o.order_date) AS order_date,
    SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS revenue
  FROM orders o
  JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
  WHERE o.order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '60 days'
  GROUP BY 1
),
with_ma AS (
  SELECT
    order_date,
    revenue,
    AVG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS sma_7d,
    AVG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 29 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS sma_30d
  FROM daily_revenue
)
SELECT
  order_date,
  revenue,
  sma_7d,
  sma_30d,
  CASE
    WHEN sma_7d > 1.2 * sma_30d THEN 'accelerating'
    WHEN sma_7d < 0.8 * sma_30d THEN 'decelerating'
    ELSE 'stable'
  END AS trend_status
FROM with_ma

mistake_patterns.mistake: Missing ORDER BY in window specification
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: Without ORDER BY, the window has no defined ordering — the moving average becomes arbitrary. Results may change between executions.
mistake_patterns.symptom: SMA values jump around or change on re-runs — not deterministic.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT AVG(revenue) OVER (ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) FROM daily_revenue
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT AVG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) FROM daily_revenue
mistake_patterns.detection_sql:
SELECT order_date, AVG(revenue) OVER (ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS sma_7d
FROM daily_revenue
ORDER BY order_date
mistake_patterns.handling_sql: Always add ORDER BY to moving averages: OVER (ORDER BY date ROWS BETWEEN N PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW).

mistake_patterns.mistake: Confusing ROWS vs RANGE for date-based windows
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: ROWS BETWEEN counts *rows*, not *days*. If data has gaps (e.g., weekends), a 7-day SMA may span 10 calendar days. RANGE between 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW doesn’t work with DATE in DuckDB/Postgres.
mistake_patterns.symptom: 7-day SMA sometimes spans 10+ days during weekends — not the intended window.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT AVG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date RANGE BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) FROM daily_revenue
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT AVG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) FROM daily_revenue
mistake_patterns.detection_sql: SELECT order_date, COUNT(*) FROM daily_revenue GROUP BY 1 HAVING COUNT(*) > 1

mistake_patterns.mistake: Ignoring warm-up period (first N rows have incomplete averages)
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: The first 6 days of a 7-day SMA have fewer than 7 rows — average is based on incomplete data and may be misleading.
mistake_patterns.symptom: SMA for first 6 days is unstable — user wants complete 7-day averages only.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT AVG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) FROM daily_revenue
mistake_patterns.good_sql:
SELECT order_date, revenue,
       AVG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS sma_7d
FROM daily_revenue
WHERE ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY order_date) > 6
mistake_patterns.detection_sql: SELECT ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY order_date), COUNT(*) FROM daily_revenue GROUP BY 1 HAVING ROW_NUMBER() <= 6

mistake_patterns.mistake: Using moving average on non-monotonic time series
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: If order_date has duplicates or is not monotonically increasing (e.g., batch loads with out-of-order timestamps), the moving average may include future values.
mistake_patterns.symptom: SMA dips when revenue should be rising — 'future' values are averaged into past averages.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT AVG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) FROM daily_revenue
mistake_patterns.good_sql:
SELECT order_date, revenue,
       AVG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date, order_id ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS sma_7d
FROM daily_revenue
ORDER BY order_date, order_id
mistake_patterns.detection_sql: SELECT order_date, COUNT(*) FROM daily_revenue GROUP BY 1 HAVING COUNT(*) > 1

dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: RANGE BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW fails with DATE — DuckDB only supports RANGE for numeric/integer ORDER BY columns.
dialect_traps.postgres_behavior: Same — RANGE requires numeric/interval ORDER BY, not DATE.
dialect_traps.bigquery_behavior: Same — RANGE only works with numeric or integer.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: Use ROWS for row-based windows. For time-based windows, pre-fill date gaps with generate_series or use a date spine.

dialect_traps.construct: DEFAULT frame when ORDER BY is present
dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: DuckDB defaults to ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW for ORDER BY — this creates cumulative sum, not moving average. For SMA, explicit frame is needed.
dialect_traps.postgres_behavior: Same — default is cumulative unless specified.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: Always specify frame explicitly: ROWS BETWEEN N PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW for SMA. Avoid relying on defaults.

choose_this_when: user wants *trailing* SMA (includes current row) for smoothing
user’s question includes 'rolling average', '7-day SMA', 'moving average'
data is dense (few gaps) and row-based windowing is acceptable
performance matters — SMA is O(n) and fast

choose_alternative_when.condition: user wants *exponential* moving average (EMA) with weighted recent points
choose_alternative_when.why: EMA weights recent points more; SMA treats all points equally

choose_alternative_when.condition: user wants *centered* moving average (look ahead and behind)
choose_alternative_when.why: Centered MA uses both leading and trailing rows; trailing MA only uses trailing

edge_cases.scenario: First N rows have incomplete window
edge_cases.what_happens: Average is computed over fewer than N rows — may be noisy or misleading.
edge_cases.handling: Filter warm-up rows: WHERE ROW_NUMBER() > N. Or use COALESCE to set default values.

edge_cases.what_happens: 7-day SMA may span 10 calendar days — row count ≠ time window.
edge_cases.handling: Generate a dense date spine and LEFT JOIN to fill gaps. Then SMA counts only business days.

edge_cases.what_happens: ORDER BY with ties may include 'future' rows in average — non-deterministic ordering.
edge_cases.handling: Add tiebreaker: ORDER BY date, order_id or ORDER BY date, ROW_NUMBER().

performance_notes.complexity: O(n log n) for sort, O(n) for SMA scan. Dominated by sort cost.
performance_notes.when_slow: Very large datasets (>10M rows) with complex ORDER BY or high-partition counts.
performance_notes.optimization_hint: Pre-filter to relevant date range before SMA. Materialize pre-aggregated daily data in a CTE. For rolling windows, consider incremental updates.

title: Z-Score Outlier Detection
when_to_use: user wants to flag data points that deviate significantly from the mean (e.g., revenue anomalies)
user’s question includes 'z-score', 'outlier detection', 'anomaly score'
validating data quality (e.g., 'is this order value plausible?')
building anomaly alerts (e.g., 'flag z-score > 3')
when_not_to_use: data is not approximately normally distributed — z-scores assume Gaussian distribution
data has heavy tails or many outliers — z-score sensitivity amplifies noise
user wants *robust* outlier detection — use IQR-based methods instead

concept_explanation.what_it_does: Z-score = (value - mean) / std_dev. It measures how many standard deviations a value is from the mean. |z| > 3 is a common anomaly threshold — implies the value is in the top/bottom 0.3% of a normal distribution.
concept_explanation.mental_model: Imagine a bell curve. Z-score tells you how far a point is from the peak in 'standard deviation units'. Z=2 means 2 standard deviations above average.
concept_explanation.key_behavior: Z-score assumes normal distribution. For skewed data, IQR-based methods are more robust. Z=0 means the value equals the mean.

template.minimal:
-- Z-score for revenue
SELECT
  order_id,
  revenue,
  (revenue - AVG(revenue) OVER ()) / NULLIF(STDDEV(revenue) OVER (), 0) AS z_score
FROM orders
template.realistic:
-- Z-score outlier detection per region, with flagging
-- Grain: one row per order with z-score
WITH order_metrics AS (
  SELECT
    c.region,
    o.order_id,
    oi.quantity * oi.unit_price AS revenue
  FROM orders o
  JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
  JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
  WHERE o.order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days'
),
region_stats AS (
  SELECT region, AVG(revenue) AS avg_rev, STDDEV(revenue) AS std_rev
  FROM order_metrics
  GROUP BY region
),
with_zscore AS (
  SELECT
    om.region,
    om.order_id,
    om.revenue,
    (om.revenue - rs.avg_rev) / NULLIF(rs.std_rev, 0) AS z_score
  FROM order_metrics om
  JOIN region_stats rs ON om.region = rs.region
)
SELECT
  region,
  order_id,
  revenue,
  ROUND(z_score, 2) AS z_score,
  CASE
    WHEN ABS(z_score) > 3 THEN 'outlier'
    WHEN ABS(z_score) > 2 THEN 'moderate'
    ELSE 'normal'
  END AS status
FROM with_zscore
ORDER BY ABS(z_score) DESC
LIMIT 100
template.composable:
-- Z-score per customer, then flag customers with >20% outliers
-- Composes: stat_zscore_outlier + agg_count
WITH order_metrics AS (
  SELECT
    c.customer_id,
    o.order_id,
    oi.quantity * oi.unit_price AS revenue
  FROM orders o
  JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
  JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
  WHERE o.order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '60 days'
),
customer_stats AS (
  SELECT
    customer_id,
    AVG(revenue) AS avg_rev,
    STDDEV(revenue) AS std_rev
  FROM order_metrics
  GROUP BY customer_id
),
with_zscore AS (
  SELECT
    om.customer_id,
    om.order_id,
    om.revenue,
    (om.revenue - cs.avg_rev) / NULLIF(cs.std_rev, 0) AS z_score
  FROM order_metrics om
  JOIN customer_stats cs ON om.customer_id = cs.customer_id
),
customer_outlier_rate AS (
  SELECT
    customer_id,
    COUNT(*) AS order_count,
    COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE ABS(z_score) > 3) AS outlier_count,
    ROUND(COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE ABS(z_score) > 3) * 100.0 / COUNT(*), 2) AS outlier_pct
  FROM with_zscore
  GROUP BY customer_id
)
SELECT * FROM customer_outlier_rate
WHERE outlier_pct > 20
ORDER BY outlier_pct DESC

mistake_patterns.mistake: Using z-score without checking normality assumption
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: Z-scores assume Gaussian distribution. For highly skewed data (e.g., power-law distributions), z > 3 may be normal, not anomalous.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Most values flagged as outliers — distribution isn’t Gaussian, z-score over-flags.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT (revenue - AVG(revenue) OVER ()) / STDDEV(revenue) OVER () AS z_score FROM orders
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT revenue FROM orders ORDER BY revenue LIMIT 100
mistake_patterns.detection_sql: SELECT AVG(revenue) AS mean, STDDEV(revenue) AS std, MIN(revenue) AS min, MAX(revenue) AS max FROM orders
mistake_patterns.handling_sql: If distribution is skewed, log-transform (e.g., LOG(revenue)) or use IQR-based methods.

mistake_patterns.mistake: Using population mean/stddev instead of group-specific
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: Global z-score mixes multiple groups (e.g., all customers). If one customer has high variance, their orders are flagged as outliers even if normal for them.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Customers with high-volume orders flagged as outliers — z-score is global, not per-customer.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT (revenue - AVG(revenue) OVER ()) / STDDEV(revenue) OVER () FROM orders
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT (revenue - AVG(revenue) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id)) / STDDEV(revenue) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id) FROM orders
mistake_patterns.detection_sql: SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id HAVING COUNT(*) > 100

mistake_patterns.mistake: Division by zero (STDDEV = 0)
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: If all values in a group are identical (e.g., all orders = $10), STDDEV = 0, causing division by zero.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Query fails with 'division by zero' or returns NULL for z-score.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT (revenue - AVG(revenue) OVER ()) / STDDEV(revenue) OVER () FROM orders
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT (revenue - AVG(revenue) OVER ()) / NULLIF(STDDEV(revenue) OVER (), 0) FROM orders
mistake_patterns.detection_sql: SELECT customer_id, STDDEV(revenue) FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id HAVING STDDEV(revenue) = 0

mistake_patterns.mistake: Confusing z-score with percentile
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: Z-score is not a percentile. z = 1.96 corresponds to ~97.5th percentile, not 96%. Users may misinterpret z = 2 as '95%ile' (it’s actually ~97.7%).
mistake_patterns.symptom: User expects z > 2 to flag top 5% of data, but it flags ~2.3% (two-tailed).
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE ABS(z_score) > 2
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE revenue > PERCENTILE_CONT(0.975) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue)
mistake_patterns.detection_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders WHERE ABS(z_score) > 2
mistake_patterns.handling_sql: For percentile-based flags, use PERCENTILE_CONT or PERCENT_RANK. Z-scores are for standard deviations, not percentiles.

dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: DuckDB supports STDDEV(x) — sqrt of variance. Z-score = (x - mean) / stddev.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: Always use STDDEV for z-score, not VARIANCE. Z = (x - μ) / σ, where σ = STDDEV.

dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: NULL values in revenue return NULL z-score — same as other aggregates.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: COALESCE(revenue, 0) or filter NULLs before z-score if NULLs are unexpected.

choose_this_when: user wants to flag *statistical outliers* based on deviation from mean
user’s question includes 'z-score', 'anomaly score', 'standard deviation'
data is approximately normally distributed
user needs *absolute* thresholds (e.g., z > 3)

choose_alternative_when.condition: data is highly skewed or has heavy outliers
choose_alternative_when.why: IQR is robust to outliers; z-score assumes normality
Percentiles are easier to interpret than z-scores for business users

edge_cases.what_happens: STDDEV = 0, z-score is NULL — no variability to measure.
edge_cases.handling: Filter out groups with STDDEV = 0, or use COALESCE to set z-score = 0.

edge_cases.what_happens: Z-score is unstable — one outlier can inflate STDDEV, masking others.
edge_cases.handling: Add minimum sample size check: HAVING COUNT(*) >= 5.

edge_cases.what_happens: Z-score per group may have low precision if groups have few rows.
edge_cases.handling: Use pooled std dev across groups, or aggregate to higher granularity first.

performance_notes.complexity: O(n) — AVG and STDDEV require one scan. Z-score is O(1) per row.
performance_notes.when_slow: None — z-score is fast. Bottlenecks are usually in data preparation.
performance_notes.optimization_hint: Pre-filter to relevant rows before z-score. Materialize group stats in a CTE if reused. For rolling z-score, use windowed AVG/STDDEV.

title: Rolling Z-Score for Time Series Anomalies
when_to_use: user wants to detect anomalies in time series data (e.g., daily revenue anomalies) where the baseline changes over time
user’s question includes 'rolling z-score', 'time-based anomaly', 'dynamic threshold'
building real-time alerting (e.g., 'flag today’s revenue if it deviates from 7-day rolling baseline')
validating recent trends without global mean (e.g., seasonality-aware anomaly detection)
when_not_to_use: user wants *static* z-score (fixed mean/std dev) — use stat_zscore_outlier instead
data has gaps or irregular spacing — rolling windows assume continuous time
user needs *global* baseline — static z-score is simpler and more stable

concept_explanation.what_it_does: Rolling z-score = (current_value - rolling_mean) / rolling_stddev. It uses a trailing window (e.g., last 7 days) to compute a dynamic baseline. Current value is *excluded* from the baseline to avoid bias (e.g., rows BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING).
concept_explanation.mental_model: Imagine a rolling window that slides across time. For each day, the window shows the last 7 days’ data, but the baseline (mean/std) is computed from those 7 days *excluding today* to avoid self-influence.
concept_explanation.key_behavior: Rolling z-score must exclude current row from baseline to avoid bias. Using CURRENT ROW in the window inflates std dev and masks outliers.

template.minimal:
-- Rolling 7-day z-score for revenue (exclude current row from baseline)
SELECT
  order_date,
  revenue,
  AVG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING) AS rolling_mean,
  STDDEV(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING) AS rolling_std,
  (revenue - AVG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING))
    / NULLIF(STDDEV(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING), 0) AS rolling_z_score
FROM daily_revenue
template.realistic:
-- Rolling 7-day z-score for daily revenue, with anomaly flags
-- Grain: one row per day with rolling z-score
WITH daily_revenue AS (
  SELECT
    DATE_TRUNC('day', o.order_date) AS order_date,
    SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS revenue
  FROM orders o
  JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
  WHERE o.order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '60 days'
  GROUP BY 1
),
with_rolling AS (
  SELECT
    order_date,
    revenue,
    AVG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING) AS rolling_mean,
    STDDEV(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING) AS rolling_std
  FROM daily_revenue
),
with_zscore AS (
  SELECT
    order_date,
    revenue,
    rolling_mean,
    rolling_std,
    (revenue - rolling_mean) / NULLIF(rolling_std, 0) AS rolling_z_score
  FROM with_rolling
)
SELECT
  order_date,
  revenue,
  rolling_mean,
  rolling_std,
  ROUND(rolling_z_score, 2) AS rolling_z_score,
  CASE
    WHEN rolling_z_score > 3 THEN 'above_upper'
    WHEN rolling_z_score < -3 THEN 'below_lower'
    ELSE 'normal'
  END AS anomaly_flag
FROM with_zscore
WHERE rolling_mean IS NOT NULL  -- exclude first 7 rows (warm-up)
ORDER BY order_date DESC
LIMIT 100
template.composable:
-- Rolling 7-day z-score, then compare to fixed percentile thresholds
-- Composes: stat_rolling_zscore + stat_percentile_distribution
WITH daily_revenue AS (
  SELECT
    DATE_TRUNC('day', o.order_date) AS order_date,
    SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS revenue
  FROM orders o
  JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
  WHERE o.order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '60 days'
  GROUP BY 1
),
percentiles AS (
  SELECT
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.05) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) AS p5_rev,
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.95) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) AS p95_rev
  FROM daily_revenue
),
with_rolling AS (
  SELECT
    order_date,
    revenue,
    AVG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING) AS rolling_mean,
    STDDEV(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING) AS rolling_std
  FROM daily_revenue
),
with_zscore AS (
  SELECT
    order_date,
    revenue,
    (revenue - rolling_mean) / NULLIF(rolling_std, 0) AS rolling_z_score
  FROM with_rolling
)
SELECT
  w.order_date,
  w.revenue,
  ROUND(w.rolling_z_score, 2) AS rolling_z_score,
  p.p5_rev,
  p.p95_rev,
  CASE
    WHEN w.revenue < p.p5_rev THEN 'below_5p'
    WHEN w.revenue > p.p95_rev THEN 'above_95p'
    WHEN w.rolling_z_score > 3 THEN 'rolling_z_high'
    WHEN w.rolling_z_score < -3 THEN 'rolling_z_low'
    ELSE 'normal'
  END AS status
FROM with_zscore w
CROSS JOIN percentiles p
WHERE w.rolling_mean IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY w.order_date DESC

mistake_patterns.mistake: Including current row in baseline (biasing the z-score)
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: If current value is included in rolling_mean/rolling_std, high or low values inflate their own baseline — z-score becomes near zero even for anomalies.
mistake_patterns.symptom: All z-scores near zero — anomalies are masked because current value defines the baseline.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT (revenue - AVG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)) / STDDEV(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) FROM daily_revenue
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT (revenue - AVG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING)) / STDDEV(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING) FROM daily_revenue
mistake_patterns.detection_sql:
SELECT order_date, revenue,
       (revenue - AVG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING)) / NULLIF(STDDEV(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING), 0) AS z_score
FROM daily_revenue
WHERE order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '7 days'
mistake_patterns.handling_sql: Always exclude current row from rolling window: ROWS BETWEEN N PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING, not CURRENT ROW.

mistake_patterns.mistake: Missing ORDER BY in window specification
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: Without ORDER BY, the rolling window has no time-based ordering — z-score uses arbitrary order, often causing false positives/negatives.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Z-scores jump around or are unrelated to actual time sequence.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT (revenue - AVG(revenue) OVER (ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING)) / STDDEV(revenue) OVER (ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING) FROM daily_revenue
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT (revenue - AVG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING)) / STDDEV(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING) FROM daily_revenue
mistake_patterns.detection_sql:
SELECT order_date, revenue,
       (revenue - AVG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING)) / NULLIF(STDDEV(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING), 0) AS z_score
FROM daily_revenue
ORDER BY order_date

mistake_patterns.mistake: Ignoring warm-up period (first N rows have incomplete baseline)
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: The first 6 days have fewer than 6 previous rows — baseline is noisy and unreliable. Flagging them as anomalies is misleading.
mistake_patterns.symptom: First 6 days flagged as outliers — warm-up period has incomplete data.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT * FROM with_zscore WHERE ABS(z_score) > 3
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT * FROM with_zscore WHERE rolling_mean IS NOT NULL AND ABS(z_score) > 3
mistake_patterns.detection_sql:
SELECT order_date, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY order_date) AS rn
FROM daily_revenue
ORDER BY order_date
LIMIT 10
mistake_patterns.handling_sql: Filter warm-up rows: WHERE rolling_mean IS NOT NULL or WHERE ROW_NUMBER() > N.

mistake_patterns.mistake: Confusing rolling z-score with static z-score
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: Rolling z-score uses *dynamic* baseline (changes daily), while static uses *fixed* global mean/std dev. Users may misinterpret rolling z-score as 'deviation from overall mean'.
mistake_patterns.symptom: User compares rolling z-scores across days and expects consistency, but baseline shifts daily.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql:
SELECT (revenue - AVG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING)) / STDDEV(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING) FROM orders
-- Static z-score:
SELECT (revenue - AVG(revenue) OVER ()) / STDDEV(revenue) OVER () FROM orders
mistake_patterns.good_sql:
SELECT revenue,
       (revenue - AVG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING)) / NULLIF(STDDEV(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING), 0) AS rolling_z,
       (revenue - AVG(revenue) OVER ()) / NULLIF(STDDEV(revenue) OVER (), 0) AS static_z
FROM orders
ORDER BY order_date
mistake_patterns.detection_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders
mistake_patterns.handling_sql: For rolling z-score, always clarify it’s time-based. For static, use OVER () without ORDER BY.

dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: DuckDB supports ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING for excluding current row.
dialect_traps.bigquery_behavior: Same — ROWS BETWEEN N PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING works.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: Always specify frame explicitly to exclude current row. Avoid using CURRENT ROW in rolling z-score windows.

dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: NULL values in revenue return NULL rolling_z_score — same as other aggregates.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: COALESCE(revenue, 0) or filter NULLs before rolling z-score if NULLs are unexpected.

choose_this_when: user wants to detect *time-based* anomalies with dynamic baseline
user’s question includes 'rolling z-score', 'time series anomaly', 'dynamic threshold'
data is continuous (no gaps) and time-ordered
user needs to avoid global baseline (e.g., seasonality-aware)

choose_alternative_when.why: Static z-score is simpler and more stable for fixed baselines

choose_alternative_when.condition: data has gaps or irregular spacing
choose_alternative_when.why: IQR is less sensitive to gaps; rolling windows assume continuity

edge_cases.scenario: First N rows have incomplete baseline
edge_cases.what_happens: Rolling z-score is NULL or noisy — baseline has fewer than N previous rows.
edge_cases.handling: Filter warm-up rows: WHERE rolling_mean IS NOT NULL or WHERE ROW_NUMBER() > N.

edge_cases.what_happens: Rolling z-score is NULL — no variability in baseline.
edge_cases.handling: Use COALESCE(rolling_std, 1) to avoid division by zero, or filter groups with zero std dev.

edge_cases.what_happens: Rolling z-score may use incorrect order if timestamps are not unique.
edge_cases.handling: Add tiebreaker: ORDER BY order_date, order_id to ensure deterministic order.

performance_notes.complexity: O(n log n) for sort, O(n) for rolling stats. Dominated by sort cost.
performance_notes.when_slow: Large datasets (>10M rows) or high-partition counts with complex ORDER BY.
performance_notes.optimization_hint: Pre-filter to relevant date range before rolling z-score. Materialize daily aggregates in a CTE. For rolling windows, consider incremental updates.

title: IQR-Based Outlier Detection
when_to_use: user wants robust outlier detection that isn’t skewed by extreme values (e.g., revenue with occasional $1M orders)
user’s question includes 'IQR outlier', 'Tukey method', 'robust anomaly'
data is skewed or has heavy tails — z-score is too sensitive
building dashboards with stable outlier thresholds
when_not_to_use: data is approximately normal — z-score is more interpretable and efficient
user wants parametric thresholds (e.g., 95% confidence) — IQR is non-parametric
data has no meaningful quartiles (e.g., binary outcomes)

concept_explanation.what_it_does: IQR = Q3 - Q1. Outliers are defined as values < Q1 - 1.5*IQR or > Q3 + 1.5*IQR. This is Tukey’s method — robust because IQR is based on medians, not means, and is insensitive to extreme values.
concept_explanation.mental_model: Imagine a box plot: the box spans Q1 to Q3 (middle 50% of data). Whiskers extend to Q1-1.5×IQR and Q3+1.5×IQR. Points outside are outliers.
concept_explanation.key_behavior: IQR-based outliers are robust to extreme values — a single $1M order won’t inflate Q3 as much as it would inflate the mean in z-score.

template.minimal:
-- IQR outlier detection
WITH stats AS (
  SELECT
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.25) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) AS q1,
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) AS q3
  FROM orders
),
outliers AS (
  SELECT
    o.*,
    s.q1,
    s.q3,
    s.q3 - s.q1 AS iqr,
    CASE
      WHEN o.revenue < s.q1 - 1.5 * (s.q3 - s.q1) THEN 'low_outlier'
      WHEN o.revenue > s.q3 + 1.5 * (s.q3 - s.q1) THEN 'high_outlier'
      ELSE 'normal'
    END AS status
  FROM orders o, stats s
)
SELECT * FROM outliers WHERE status != 'normal'
template.realistic:
-- IQR outlier detection per region, with robust thresholds
-- Grain: one row per order with IQR-based status
WITH order_metrics AS (
  SELECT
    c.region,
    o.order_id,
    oi.quantity * oi.unit_price AS revenue
  FROM orders o
  JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
  JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
  WHERE o.order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days'
),
region_stats AS (
  SELECT
    region,
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.25) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) AS q1,
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) AS q3
  FROM order_metrics
  GROUP BY region
),
with_iqr AS (
  SELECT
    om.region,
    om.order_id,
    om.revenue,
    rs.q1,
    rs.q3,
    rs.q3 - rs.q1 AS iqr
  FROM order_metrics om
  JOIN region_stats rs ON om.region = rs.region
)
SELECT
  region,
  order_id,
  revenue,
  ROUND(q1, 2) AS q1,
  ROUND(q3, 2) AS q3,
  ROUND(iqr, 2) AS iqr,
  CASE
    WHEN revenue < q1 - 1.5 * iqr THEN 'low_outlier'
    WHEN revenue > q3 + 1.5 * iqr THEN 'high_outlier'
    ELSE 'normal'
  END AS status
FROM with_iqr
ORDER BY CASE WHEN status = 'normal' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END, revenue DESC
LIMIT 100
template.composable:
-- IQR outlier detection, then compare to z-score thresholds for combined flagging
-- Composes: stat_iqr_outlier + stat_zscore_outlier
WITH order_metrics AS (
  SELECT
    c.region,
    o.order_id,
    oi.quantity * oi.unit_price AS revenue
  FROM orders o
  JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
  JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
  WHERE o.order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days'
),
region_stats AS (
  SELECT
    region,
    AVG(revenue) AS avg_rev,
    STDDEV(revenue) AS std_rev,
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.25) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) AS q1,
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) AS q3
  FROM order_metrics
  GROUP BY region
),
with_both AS (
  SELECT
    om.region,
    om.order_id,
    om.revenue,
    (om.revenue - rs.avg_rev) / NULLIF(rs.std_rev, 0) AS z_score,
    rs.q1,
    rs.q3,
    rs.q3 - rs.q1 AS iqr
  FROM order_metrics om
  JOIN region_stats rs ON om.region = rs.region
)
SELECT
  region,
  order_id,
  revenue,
  ROUND(z_score, 2) AS z_score,
  CASE
    WHEN revenue < q1 - 1.5 * iqr THEN 'IQR_outlier'
    WHEN revenue > q3 + 1.5 * iqr THEN 'IQR_outlier'
    ELSE 'IQR_normal'
  END AS iqr_status,
  CASE
    WHEN ABS(z_score) > 3 THEN 'z_outlier'
    ELSE 'z_normal'
  END AS z_status
FROM with_both
WHERE revenue < q1 - 1.5 * iqr OR revenue > q3 + 1.5 * iqr OR ABS(z_score) > 3

mistake_patterns.mistake: Using IQR without verifying non-parametric assumptions
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: IQR assumes the data has a meaningful median and quartiles. For binary data (0/1) or highly bimodal distributions, Q1/Q3 may not reflect the true 'center'.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Outlier thresholds don’t match business logic — e.g., IQR flags 0 as an outlier when 0 is normal.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE revenue < PERCENTILE_CONT(0.25) - 1.5 * IQR
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT DISTINCT revenue FROM orders ORDER BY revenue
mistake_patterns.detection_sql: SELECT MIN(revenue), MAX(revenue), COUNT(DISTINCT revenue) FROM orders
mistake_patterns.handling_sql: For binary/bimodal data, use IQR only if median makes sense. Otherwise, use domain-specific thresholds.

mistake_patterns.mistake: Using IQR on grouped data without group-specific thresholds
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: Global IQR mixes multiple groups. If one group has high revenue, its Q3 inflates the global threshold — outliers in low-revenue groups are missed.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Low-revenue orders are not flagged as outliers — global IQR is too wide.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE revenue > PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) + 1.5 * IQR
mistake_patterns.good_sql:
SELECT * FROM orders o
WHERE revenue > (
  SELECT PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) + 1.5 * (PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) - PERCENTILE_CONT(0.25))
  FROM orders WHERE customer_id = o.customer_id
)
mistake_patterns.detection_sql: SELECT region, COUNT(*) FROM orders GROUP BY region HAVING COUNT(*) > 1000
mistake_patterns.handling_sql: Precompute per-group stats in a CTE.

mistake_patterns.mistake: Using IQR thresholds without checking for zero IQR
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: If all values in a group are identical (e.g., all orders = $10), IQR = 0, and threshold becomes Q1 - 0 = Q1 — no outliers are possible, even if data should vary.
mistake_patterns.symptom: No outliers flagged — IQR = 0, thresholds collapse to Q1.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE revenue > q3 + 1.5 * iqr AND iqr > 0
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE revenue > q3 + 1.5 * NULLIF(iqr, 0) OR iqr = 0 AND revenue != q1
mistake_patterns.detection_sql:
SELECT region, q1, q3, q3 - q1 AS iqr FROM (
  SELECT region, PERCENTILE_CONT(0.25) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) AS q1, PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) AS q3
  FROM orders
  GROUP BY region
) t
WHERE iqr = 0
mistake_patterns.handling_sql: Use NULLIF(iqr, 0) and add fallback: CASE WHEN iqr = 0 THEN ABS(revenue - q1) > threshold ELSE revenue outside [q1-1.5*iqr, q3+1.5*iqr] END.

mistake_patterns.mistake: Confusing IQR with standard deviation outlier thresholds
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: IQR uses 1.5×IQR for outliers, while z-score uses 3σ. For normal data, 1.5×IQR ≈ 2.7σ — slightly more sensitive than z-score’s 3σ. Users may misinterpret IQR thresholds.
mistake_patterns.symptom: More outliers flagged with IQR than z-score — not an error, but unexpected.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE revenue > q3 + 3 * iqr
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE revenue > q3 + 1.5 * iqr
mistake_patterns.detection_sql:
SELECT q1, q3, q3 - q1 AS iqr, COUNT(*) FROM (
  SELECT revenue,
         PERCENTILE_CONT(0.25) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) OVER () AS q1,
         PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) OVER () AS q3
  FROM orders
) WHERE revenue > q3 + 1.5 * iqr
mistake_patterns.handling_sql: For normal data, z-score thresholds (3σ) are tighter. For skewed data, IQR is more robust. Choose based on distribution, not habit.

dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: DuckDB supports PERCENTILE_CONT for Q1/Q3. IQR = Q3 - Q1.
dialect_traps.postgres_behavior: Postgres supports PERCENTILE_CONT. IQR is easy to compute.
dialect_traps.bigquery_behavior: BigQuery supports PERCENTILE_CONT — same as DuckDB/Postgres.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: IQR is portable — use PERCENTILE_CONT(0.25) and PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) to compute Q1/Q3. No dialect-specific issues.

dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: PERCENTILE_CONT ignores NULLs — same as other aggregates.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: No change needed — PERCENTILE_CONT handles NULLs consistently. But verify with COUNT(*) vs COUNT(column) to detect NULLs.

choose_this_when: user wants *robust* outlier detection for skewed or outlier-heavy data
user’s question includes 'IQR', 'Tukey', 'robust anomaly'
data has heavy tails or extreme values that distort z-score
non-parametric thresholds are preferred (no distribution assumptions)

choose_alternative_when.condition: data is approximately normal and z-score is interpretable
choose_alternative_when.why: Z-scores are more interpretable (standard deviations from mean)
Z-scores align with normal distribution confidence intervals

edge_cases.what_happens: IQR = 0, Q1 = Q3 = median — no outliers possible.
edge_cases.handling: Add fallback: flag values ≠ median as outliers, or use coefficient of variation (CV = 0 means no variability).

edge_cases.what_happens: Q1/Q3 may be unstable — quartiles require at least 4 points for meaningful separation.
edge_cases.handling: Add minimum sample size check: HAVING COUNT(*) >= 4.

edge_cases.what_happens: IQR is correct, but outlier thresholds may exclude values that are actually normal.
edge_cases.handling: No issue — IQR handles ties correctly. Verify with visual inspection if needed.

performance_notes.complexity: O(n log n) for percentile computation, O(n) for outlier classification. Dominated by sort cost.
performance_notes.when_slow: Large datasets (>10M rows) or high-partition counts.
performance_notes.optimization_hint: Pre-filter to relevant rows before computing percentiles. Materialize group stats in a CTE if reused. For rolling IQR, use windowed PERCENTILE_CONT.
